title: Minnesota Sues Federal Government for Evidence in Three ICE Shootings
excerpt: Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison and Hennepin County Attorney Mary Moriarty filed a federal lawsuit accusing the Trump administration of 'categorically withholding evidence' in the fatal shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti and the non-fatal shooting of Julio Cesar Sosa-Celis, calling the practice 'unprecedented in American history.'
content:
On March 24, 2026, the Minnesota Attorney General's Office, Hennepin County Attorney's Office, and the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension filed suit in U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia against the Department of Justice and Department of Homeland Security.

  • The lawsuit alleged that the federal government had 'adopted a policy of categorically withholding evidence' — including the names of agents involved, bodycam footage, personnel files, and physical evidence — preventing state authorities from conducting independent criminal investigations into three shootings.
  • Key withheld evidence included Pretti's cell phone, Good's car (described as shrink-wrapped in an FBI warehouse and never examined), and the training and personnel files of every agent involved in the shootings. Hennepin County Attorney Moriarty explicitly asked: 'When was [agent Ross] trained? What was he trained to do? What's in his personnel file?'
  • The lawsuit argued the federal government's blanket refusal violated the Administrative Procedure Act and Minnesota's 10th Amendment police powers.
  • DHS acknowledged for the first time in response to the lawsuit that all three incidents — including Good's killing, which officials had previously claimed was not under investigation — remained under active investigation.

The filing marked what Moriarty called an 'escalation unprecedented in American history,' reflecting the complete breakdown of federal-state law enforcement cooperation in the aftermath of Operation Metro Surge.
mediaPlaceholder: Court filing documents and press conference with Minnesota AG Ellison and Hennepin County Attorney Moriarty
